question: What happens when merging two interfaces with the same property but different types?

options.text: TypeScript produces a compilation error — property types must match during merging
TypeScript automatically creates a union type from both declarations
options.explanation: TypeScript does NOT automatically create unions. Properties during merging must have identical types, otherwise it's an error

options.text: The last declaration overwrites the property type
options.explanation: Interface merging doesn't overwrite properties — all declarations must be compatible. Overwriting is intersection (&) behavior, not merging

options.text: TypeScript ignores the second declaration
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't ignore repeated declarations — it tries to merge them. If types conflict, an error occurs

question: How do you correctly extend third-party module types through module augmentation?

options.text: Create a file with import, then declare module 'library' { interface ExistingInterface { ... } }
Simply write declare module 'library' { ... } in any file without import
options.explanation: A file without import/export is a global script, not a module. Module augmentation works correctly only from module files

options.text: Use a type alias with intersection: type Extended = Original & { newProp: string }
options.explanation: Intersection creates a new type but doesn't extend the original. Other code using Original won't see newProp

options.text: Edit the file in node_modules directly
options.explanation: Changes in node_modules are lost on npm install. Module augmentation is the proper way to extend types without modifying source

question: Which declarations can merge with each other in TypeScript?

options.text: Interface + Interface
Namespace + Namespace
Class + Namespace
Type Alias + Type Alias
options.explanation: Type aliases (type X = ...) never merge. Redeclaring is a Duplicate identifier error. Use interface for merging

options.text: Class + Class
options.explanation: Two classes with the same name in the same scope is an error. Classes don't merge, unlike interface + interface

question: What is the overload order when merging two blocks of the same interface?

options.text: Later block overloads have higher priority, but string literals are hoisted to the top
First block overloads always have higher priority
options.explanation: It's the opposite: later blocks have higher priority. TypeScript considers later definitions more specific

options.text: TypeScript sorts overloads by parameter count
options.explanation: Order doesn't depend on parameter count. It's determined by block order and presence of string literals

options.text: Overloads are merged in random order
options.explanation: Order is strictly defined: within a block — original order, between blocks — later has priority, literals — hoisted

question: Why is declare global needed in a file with import/export?

options.text: To extend global types (Window, Array, etc.) from a module file
To create global variables accessible in all files
options.explanation: declare global doesn't create runtime variables — it only describes types. Runtime globals require a different approach

options.text: To disable strict mode for a code block
options.explanation: declare global doesn't affect strict mode. It's used exclusively for extending the global type namespace

options.text: To export types to all project files without import
options.explanation: declare global extends already existing global interfaces. For regular type exports, use export
